title: Floor loom dressing step by step
subtitle: How to wind the warp, thread the heddles, sley the reed, and tie on ready to weave
excerpt: Dressing a floor loom is the full process of preparing it for weaving: winding the warp on a warping board, transferring it to the loom, threading each warp end through its heddle, sleying the reed, and tying on to the front beam. Each stage must be done in order and with consistent tension for the finished cloth to weave evenly.
sourceNotes: Floor loom dressing is documented consistently across all four-shaft loom manufacturer manuals and in standard UK and US weaving reference books. The back-to-front method described is the most widely taught approach.

glossaryTerms.definition: The complete process of preparing a floor loom for weaving: winding the warp to the correct length and thread count, transferring it to the loom, threading through the heddles, sleying through the reed, and tying on to the front and back beams. Also called 'dressing the loom'.

Threading the warp ends through the dents of the reed in the correct order and at the correct sett. The number of ends per dent is calculated from the planned sett and the dents-per-centimetre rating of the reed. Sleying distributes the warp threads across the weaving width.

A short loop or wire with a central eye through which one warp thread passes. Each heddle is attached to a shaft; raising the shaft lifts all heddles (and their threads) on that shaft simultaneously, opening the shed. The threading sequence across the shafts determines the weave structure.

aliases: how to warp a floor loom

content.text: is the most time-intensive part of weaving, but each stage is methodical rather than complex. Threading each warp end through its

through the reed requires attention and patience but not weaving experience. A well-dressed loom produces cloth with consistent tension across the full width. Threading mistakes and uneven sleying are visible in the finished cloth and cannot be corrected once weaving has started.

items.name: Warp yarn for the planned project
Four-shaft floor loom with heddles loaded on all shafts
Warping board for measuring warp length
Reed of the correct dent size for the planned sett
Threading hook for pulling ends through heddle eyes
Sleying hook for pulling ends through reed dents
Lease sticks (two flat sticks) for maintaining thread order

content.text: Wind the calculated number of warp ends on a warping board to the planned warp length. Count threads carefully and include a cross at one end: the cross (made by winding figure-of-eight around the last two pegs) preserves the thread order for threading. When the warp is complete, choke-tie the cross with a short length of contrast yarn through both sides, then chain the warp off the board.

Thread the lease sticks through the cross at the chained end of the warp, then remove the choke ties. Thread the lease sticks into the loom through the back, slide the warp over the back beam, and begin winding onto the warp beam. Insert sticks, paper, or cardboard between layers as you wind to prevent the lower layers from pressing unevenly into the threads above. Wind with firm, even tension, combing through the threads toward the front of the loom as each section is wound.

Working from the centre of the warp outward (or right to left, following the threading draft), take each warp end in order from the lease sticks and pull it through the eye of the correct heddle using a threading hook. Check the threading draft for each end before hooking it through. After every 10 to 20 ends, count back to verify the order is correct: threading errors are much easier to fix at this stage than after sleying.

Slide the reed into the beater and sley the warp ends through the dents in order at the planned sett. For a balanced plain weave at 12 ends per 10 cm with a 12-dent reed, place one end in each dent. Working from the centre outward helps keep the warp centred in the reed. When sleying is complete, tie the warp ends in groups of 6 to 8 to the front apron rod with a surgeon's knot, adjusting the tension on each group until the full warp feels consistent.

items.problem: A threading error is found after sleying and tying on
items.cause: One or more ends were pulled through the wrong heddle during threading
items.fix: Untie the affected section, pull the misthreaded ends back through the reed, correct the heddle threading, and re-sley. Check the three ends on each side of the error to confirm none are also affected.

items.problem: The tension is uneven across the warp with slack threads at the edges
items.cause: The apron rod ties were not adjusted evenly during tying on
items.fix: Untie and re-tie the slack groups, pulling slightly more tension on each. Check by pressing the back of one hand across the full warp width: all threads should resist with the same firmness.
